title: The Richard Linklater Hall of Fame
format: Sean, Amanda, and Chris Ryan go film by film through Richard Linklater's catalog and settle on a 10-film Hall of Fame. Plus: Sean talks to Ron Howard about 'Jim Henson: Idea Man' and his run of documentaries.

segments.heading: Before the shrine: a box-office check-in
segments.note: Furiosa, Anyone But You, and Edward Norton's deck company

films.title: Furiosa: A Mad Max Saga
films.note: The soft opening that set off the doom talk; Amanda saw it advertised as 'Bleu & Co.' on posters in Paris. Sean says the business has changed and everyone knows it, which is depressing but done.

films.title: Anyone But You
films.note: The rom-com everyone admits isn't that good but watched anyway; its run is chalked up to Sydney Sweeney TikTok. On the plane it was the movie everyone but Sean chose.

films.title: Challengers
films.note: Made $86 million; cited as the reason to wait a beat before declaring the year a disaster.

films.title: The Fall Guy
films.note: More people wanted it last Tuesday than the Tuesday before, ten days into digital, which nobody can explain.

segments.heading: The Richard Linklater Hall of Fame

films.title: Slacker
films.note: The Austin breakthrough and the totem of underground culture that hooked a 12-year-old Chris. In on the first ballot as the origin point.

films.title: Dazed and Confused
films.note: Sean's six-star classic and the hangout template every later Linklater keeps chasing. Locked.

films.title: Before Sunrise
films.note: The Vienna walk-and-talk; a sincere movie about being 23. The first of the trilogy, in.

films.title: School of Rock
films.note: His biggest, most conventional hit ($130M on a $35M budget), written by Mike White. The 'from the director of' calling card. Green.

films.title: Before Sunset
films.note: The group's near-consensus favorite: a sequel that elevates the original, with the 'you go by, you go by' car scene and one of the best endings in movies.

films.title: A Scanner Darkly
films.note: The rotoscope they kept over Waking Life; Philip K. Dick surveillance paranoia with Downey, Keanu, Harrelson, and Ryder. The one animated slot.

films.title: Before Midnight
films.note: The hard, brave one: a perfect couple gnawing at each other a decade on, the hotel-room fight and all. Oscar-nominated screenplay, only now becoming resonant for Sean.

films.title: Boyhood
films.note: Shot over 12 years; an aimless-by-design experiment that won Patricia Arquette an Oscar and should have been Linklater's year. Absolutely in.

films.title: Everybody Wants Some!!
films.note: The under-seen college-baseball hangout the show evangelizes; a spiritual Dazed follow-up that turns out to be about much more than busting balls.

films.title: Hit Man
films.note: The Glen Powell / Skip Hollandsworth story; a recent film is hard to induct, but they call it a brave choice and expect it to keep rising past the rotoscopes and Bernie.

segments.heading: The ones left out
segments.note: Debated film by film and cut

films.title: SubUrbia
films.note: The dark side of Dazed, from Eric Bogosian's play; Ribisi and Steve Zahn are good, but it's too misanthropic for a filmmaker with an earnest core. Out, with respect.

films.title: The Newton Boys
films.note: His big Hollywood period-gangster swing, with Altman's Thieves Like Us in its DNA; the movie of his that works least well. Out.

films.title: Waking Life
films.note: The first rotoscope experiment, a reel of filmed conversations and ruminations reuniting Wiley Wiggins. Lost the animated slot to A Scanner Darkly.

films.title: Tape
films.note: The real-time one-room Steven Belber play with Ethan Hawke and Uma Thurman; a fascinating experiment that hasn't aged as well as you'd want. Red.

films.title: Bad News Bears
films.note: A rare complete miss. The original is perfect; Billy Bob's fine, but the remake is the red-meat end of one-for-them. Out.

films.title: Fast Food Nation
films.note: The Eric Schlosser adaptation and polemic, sprawling and Altman-esque; noble, bracing in spots, but it doesn't land. First of four Glen Powell collaborations. Red.

films.title: Me and Orson Welles
films.note: Overlooked old-Hollywood charmer with Christian McKay as Welles and a strong Zac Efron; pretty good, not Hall of Fame. Nearly skipped entirely.

films.title: Inning by Inning: A Portrait of a Coach
films.note: His rare documentary, on Texas's Augie Garrido, the winningest coach in college baseball history. Not in.

films.title: Bernie
films.note: The Texas true-crime dark comedy with Jack Black, McConaughey, and Shirley MacLaine, carried by its small-town Greek chorus. From another Skip Hollandsworth story; a yellow that just missed.

films.title: Last Flag Flying
films.note: The downbeat Amazon road drama with Carell, Fishburne, and Cranston; his Manchester by the Sea that doesn't quite work, maybe a casting problem. Red.

films.title: Where'd You Go, Bernadette
films.note: The Cate Blanchett adaptation critics savaged; the hosts found it fine, even charming, more a Billy Crudup movie than a Blanchett one. Out.

films.title: Apollo 10½: A Space Age Childhood
films.note: The Netflix rotoscoped memory piece about a Houston kid and the moon landing, with Glen Powell; overlooked, and the hosts admit they were a little too kind to it on air. Red.

segments.heading: Ron Howard on 'Jim Henson: Idea Man'

films.title: Jim Henson: Idea Man
films.note: Howard's Disney+ documentary; he opens on a melancholy Henson photo shoot and leans on a late-arriving Jane Henson audio tape and Lisa Henson's high-school photos to build the family story alongside the Muppets origin.

films.title: Thirteen Lives
films.note: His Thai cave-rescue film, which he says the doc work directly shaped: less theatrical, more observed. He thinks it didn't find its audience because of when it came out.

films.title: Eden
films.note: The cautionary true-crime thriller he'd just finished shooting while cutting Jim Henson: 'a schizophrenic creative year.'

films.title: Rebuilding Paradise
films.note: His documentary on the Paradise, California fire; the raw emotion his cameras caught there informed how he staged the parents and divers in 13 Lives.

films.title: The Beatles: Eight Days a Week - The Touring Years
films.note: The touring-years doc; on the last day of interviews, Lynn Oliver's account of the Beatles refusing to play a segregated concert changed the whole structure.

films.title: Harlan County U.S.A.
films.note: His 'last great thing,' Barbara Kopple's 1976 labor documentary, which he finally watched all the way through on a Delta flight and found still 100% relevant.

Inning by Inning: A Portrait of a Coach.title: Inning by Inning: A Portrait of a Coach

The Beatles: Eight Days a Week - The Tou.title: The Beatles: Eight Days a Week - The Touring Years
